Zitat_AboutMe: I am enthusiastic about developing new concepts, designing inspiring spaces and bringing innovative ideas to life with dedication. Exploring new avenues and finding creative solutions are a constant source of motivation for me.
text_AboutMe:
I am and I am a 3D Artist.
After my study have gained a wide range of experience - from trade fair and event architecture at the PR agency to high-quality interior design in the luxury segment, to office projects and flagship stores at the well-known office.
In all these areas, 3D visualization was a key tool for communicating complex design ideas in a clear and atmospherically convincing way.
I am proficient in common 3D software, architectural planning programs, and Adobe Creative Suite. My keen sense of composition, light, and materiality helps me develop both photorealistic and stylized representations. I confidently translate technical planning documents and CAD data into visual concepts.
In addition to my design skills, previous project partners particularly appreciated my structured and reliable way of working - whether working independently or in agile, interdisciplinary teams.
I look forward to new contacts and exciting exchanges!
text_contact: Please share a little bit about yourself. Fill out this form so I can get to know you and your needs. I aim to respond to your inquiry within 24-48 hours.
text_contact_Problem: The main problem we’re trying to solve is
text_kitchen_intro: A design statement in the interplay between historic substance and contemporary interior design.
text_kitchen_block1:
In this historic apartment in the heart of Venice, original architectural substance blends with a bold, contemporary design concept. The kitchen becomes the central room—both functional and atmospheric. The terrazzo flooring deliberately references Venetian building tradition, anchoring the project in its historic context. At the same time, it forms a calm, mineral base for the color and material concept above.
The view of the canal and the plant-filled balcony significantly define the space.
text_kitchen_block2:
The green of the vegetation is translated into the interior as a design motif, finding its continuation in the green kitchen counter—acting as a visual link between architecture, landscape, and interior.
The open dining area is clearly distinguishable from the kitchen zone yet remains spatially connected. The red color palette is only cited in minimalist accents. Dominating the space are wood surfaces, warm earth tones, and a graphic wallpaper that lend the room a tactile and atmospheric quality.
text_kitchen_block3: Purposefully placed design classics act as conceptual anchor points: lighting by Ingo Maurer and seating by Thonet complement the ensemble, reflecting a design approach that values timelessness, quality, and cultural reference.
text_donuts: Donuts, Sprinkles & Icing - A Tribute to Dessert.
comp2_block1:
The digital presentation of the Torno Chair by +Halle aims to highlight the furniture’s iconic design language through an interplay of geometry, color, and materiality.
The visual composition was consistently derived from the chair’s DNA; its striking circular form is reflected throughout the set design.
comp2_block2:
The result is a harmonious visual language that frames the object without competing with it.
The chair serves as a monochromatic focal point, and its intense color makes a clear statement in the room. In addition, the lighting emphasizes the sculptural quality of the design and allows the chair’s curves to stand out vividly.
comp2_block3: In a deliberate close-up, the focus is on the exciting interplay of textures. The rough, tactile feel of the fabric upholstery meets the cool precision of the smooth surfaces.
text_comp1_intro: Two Acts of Being - A digital homage to the tactile moments of life.
text_comp1_block1: The kitchen table is far more than a mere surface; it is the vibrant center of gathering, a silent witness to our daily rituals. In this series, the table becomes the stage for a fleeting snapshot. Inspired by the tradition of classical still lifes, these compositions capture the beauty of the unhurried and pause for a moment before the moment passes again in the flow of everyday life.
text_comp1_block2:
No.1 - Here, the timeless durability of silver travertine meets the fragility of the moment. The play of light and shadow on and through crystal-clear glasses and their contents, along with the tactile honesty of the food, speak of a pause.
No.2 - The warmth of familiar hours and sweet gatherings. The deep grain of mango wood, the golden sheen of a cinnamon roll, and the delicate
text_comp1_block3:
blooming of fresh flowers frame the daily ritual of drinking coffee.
A soft play of shadows envelops the scene in a nostalgia that reminds us of those precious moments.
text_meta_intro: SURREAL NARRATIVE - an abstract, visually striking story that unfolds through shifting atmospheres and transitions from day to night.
text_meta_block1:
This stylized series of images was created as part of the final project at the OFG and comprises ten rendered scenes.
At the center of the narrative lies a sunken courtyard with a fountain, nestled in a surreal landscape of dark rocks and pastel-colored grasses. Over the course of the sequence, this space is transformed by the intervention of a sculptural force, accompanied by dynamic lighting effects and visual staging.
The sequence opens with a tracking shot through a rocky, abstract terrain, traversed by a bluish-shimmering river and surrounded by pastel-colored grasses. The viewer’s gaze is drawn to a sunken courtyard framed by a colonnade of round arches that blends seamlessly into the architectural language.
text_meta_block2:
In the heart of the courtyard stands a fountain.
Suddenly, the water in the fountain begins to rise—as if moved by an invisible force. The reason for this unexpected movement: a golden sculpture rises from within the fountain. It begins to stretch toward the sky in a spiral. As it moves, the water level rises as well, higher and higher, until everything is finally submerged. Finally, the mass attempts to break free but is held back by an invisible membrane. In a dramatic moment, the sculpture pierces the dome of water, causing the water to spread explosively into the surrounding landscape. As the shell breaks, luminous particles are hurled into the sky from the tip of the sculpture. They scatter across the night sky; the moon appears and bathes the scene in a mysterious, cinematic light.
text_meta_block3:
Atmospheric shifts in light: From natural daylight to moonlit night Contrasts in material & texture: Hard vs. soft, cool vs. warm – this choice of materials creates visual depth and an exciting balance between tension and harmony. Strong visual language: Each image functions as a self-contained chapter within the narrative.
The design goal is an immersive, narrative-driven visual space that makes the process of transformation through light, material, and form tangible.

Project at the OFG
text_tfh_intro: Waterkant - Fluid forms echo the surrounding waters of the port city and meet the clean lines of the existing architecture in the new store.
text_tfh_block1:
The wave-shaped ceiling installation seems to float as it winds its way through the space, supporting the zoning and flow of movement while also creating a natural counterbalance to the stepped room layout.
Mirrored surfaces serve as highlights, mimicking the appearance of water and reflecting light and the surroundings. Regionally designed graphics and special materials such as shipping container sheet metal bring the cityscape unmistakably into the interior, allowing the brand and location to blend seamlessly. Through the generous, floor-to-ceiling storefront windows of the corner building, the interior design becomes visible and tangible from the outside, establishing the store as a flagship location.
The “clear shell,” a constant feature of Telekom’s retail design, frames the entire interior with black ceilings and walls and makes the Magenta brand world shine.
text_tfh_block2:
Spectacular: The newly designed staircase, which glows entirely in magenta and conveys the brand’s identity both inside and out through translucent side panels. As a central element, it provides the welcome counter with a dramatic backdrop that subtly showcases the theme of water through dynamic lighting effects.
Green Magenta – In the Hamburg flagship store, the theme of sustainability also becomes part of the experiential world. The shop concept deliberately focuses on sustainability and environmental awareness, for example through the use of recycled and Cradle to Cradle-certified materials. A green ceiling deliberately highlights the central welcome area on the ground floor and enhances the atmosphere and indoor climate. The hanging plants are visible from the outside and visually connect the floors inside.
text_tfh_block3:
A vibrant experience – The dynamic interior design and visionary ideas for a distinctive presentation of the brand, products, service, and consultation make a clear statement at the “Waterkant.” They demonstrate what sustainable retail design can—and must—achieve. The ability to host workshops and events on the upper floor also contributes to the shop’s popularity. A Hamburg-inspired coffee bar invites you to linger. Whether it’s the seagull lights, the shipping container wall, or the wallpaper featuring local motifs, the theme of Hamburg is subtly woven throughout the store. Additionally, the meeting room can be opened up to the event area, making it multifunctional.

office: Lepel & Lepel

photos: HGEsch
text_pearl_intro: Luxury meets maritime design—fluid forms echo the surrounding water and create a unique design.
text_pearl_block1:
The studio a.s.h. firm oversaw the design, construction, and completion of the yacht. Organic forms evoking the movement of water, along with silver, mother-of-pearl, and blue tones reflecting the understated elegance of shells, fish, and other marine life, bring the ocean indoors.
With various milling and foil patterns and an exclusive selection of materials, the design receives a distinctive finishing touch. In the hallway and the owner’s cabin, for example, a milling pattern is used that is vaguely reminiscent of coral. This milled pattern is repeated, for instance, on the headboard or the cabinet front.
text_pearl_block2:
The design incorporates both single and double layers—the wall cladding becomes a work of art. The smooth milled panel, finished in Iriodin Icy White Shimmer, stands out beautifully against the silver Midas surface and, when backlit in the hallway, creates an exciting ambient light and eye-catching feature in the stairwell.
This principle is also applied to the bar counter on the deck. During the day, it appears as a self-contained unit with a minimalist pattern; in the evening, it becomes an eye-catcher that gently diffuses indirect light through its translucent patterns.
text_pearl_block3:
The milled patterns are complemented by various foil designs. A printed metallic foil resembling fish scales or a cutout that mimics the shadows of a sandy seabed—the maritime theme playfully runs through the yacht’s surfaces.

Office: studio a.s.h.

Tools used to create the milling files: Rhino & Grasshopper
Tools used to create the films: Photoshop & Illustrator
text_horten_Intro: Hortensia Chair by Moooi – an everyday object that, through its form and materials, becomes a work of art in its own right.
text_horten_block1: The extraordinary surface made of textile blossoms gives the armchair an almost fairy-tale-like effect. The goal was to capture the movement and diversity of the structure, as well as to make the sensuality of the object visually tangible. The delicate pink of the armchair creates a striking color contrast to the bold blues and dark tones of the surroundings.
text_horten_block2: This harmonious, monochromatic design allows the main object to stand out. The complementary objects from the contemporary interior design world contribute to the visual impact without overshadowing the central motif. The variety of materials creates a sense of depth and brings the image to life.
text_horten_block3:
Lighting design plays a central role in the staging of the still life. Natural sunlight combined with a subtle play of shadows cast by simulated leaves creates a soft, atmospheric mood.

Project at OFG
Tools: 3ds Max & Arnold
comp2_Wohnen_intro: With strong visual connections and an open floor plan, the residence takes on a modern, loft-like character:
comp2_Wohnen_block1:
In Müngersdorf, the task was to develop a new design for an existing residential building. The main objective was to transform the rigid and unflattering floor plan into a modern and spacious living area.
Thanks to its spaciousness, the entryway features not only storage space but also a high-quality seating area. The mirrored wall playfully enlarges the entry area and draws attention to the textured wallpaper, which lends a cozy feel to the passageway.
comp2_Wohnen_block2:
The open floor plan allows the eye to glide all the way into the living room—the kitchen island serves as a connecting element here. As the centerpiece, it is lighter in color than the rest of the kitchen and stands out skillfully. A bar with pocket doors and a pull-out work surface is planned as a partition to the entryway.
The living room is divided into a spacious dining and living area.
comp2_Wohnen_block3:
The dark wallpaper in the living room provides a striking contrast to the predominantly light design and is also a real eye-catcher to the touch. The recurring contrasts in dark blue tones serve as focal points in the designed spaces and redefine the visual emphasis.

Office: studio a.s.h.
Software: SketchUp & VRAY
text_Wasser_intro: An exploration of water and architecture—through selected installations and details, a harmonious interplay of movement and stillness emerges—a new archetype seems to be born.
text_Wasser_block1:
Beginning with a spring, a cycle is created that flows through the various spaces and modes of presentation, guiding and accompanying the visitor.
The building itself consists of four visible floors, arranged in the manner of river stones stacked one upon another. The stacked elements, with a generous number of projections and recesses, create a spacious main room in the center while also enabling the zoning of the spaces. The central element is the circulation zone, which is equipped with a circular elevator that runs through the three main floors. It serves as the focal point and anchor.
Upon entering the building, one first passes through a spacious forecourt into a light-filled atrium.
text_Wasser_block2:
This is the arrival and reception level—it houses a café, the front desk, and the generously designed circulation area.
The first floor is dedicated to both permanent and temporary exhibitions. Analog immersive installations provide an artistic exploration of the theme. Here, too, the metaphorical stream guides visitors from one room to the next, from inside to outside and back again.
On the second floor is a thermal bath that allows visitors to experience the various textures. From the cold plunge pool and Jacuzzi to the saltwater and swimming pools or sauna—in short: an intense exploration against the backdrop of conscious perception.
text_Wasser_block3:
To find peace, the relaxation room offers lounging areas with an impressive view. The view of the valley brings the surroundings into the room and ensures that one can let their thoughts wander in a relaxed manner.
The attic forms the framework for the entire installation. The main focus is on the waterway that connects the various zones of the roof. It leads from the access zone across the roof to the green roof, where ample seating invites visitors to linger. In the access zone, the artificial stream flows downward, creating a connection between the floors.

Tools: Revit & Lumion
biagio_intro: Biagio, designed in 1968 by Tobia Scarpa, is distributed by the lighting manufacturer Flos. The lamp captivates with its organic form and, at the same time, its simple, geometric basic structure.
biagio_block1: What makes it special: It is crafted from a single block of Carrara marble. Tobias Scarpa, son of the renowned architect Carlo Scarpa, views design as an intuitive, well-thought-out process.
biagio_block2: His works are characterized by minimalist forms that harmoniously combine tradition and innovation. The Biagio lamp is an expression of this philosophy: a functional object with a sculptural character.
chess_intro: Chess set – clean lines and high-quality materials create a modern and timeless look.
chess_block1: The game board, made of stained wooden blocks in light and dark tones, creates tension and depth through subtle variations in height. The board is framed by an anthracite-colored metal frame. Bronze-finished letters mark the squares and add an elegant touch. The chess pieces are crafted from natural stone (quartzite)—Quartzite Nero for the black pieces and Quartzite Bianco for the white pieces.
chess_block2:
The king and queen are additionally adorned with bronze details, creating a design connection to the materiality of the board markings. The contrast between hard (stone, metal) and soft (wood) materials creates an exciting yet harmonious overall look.
In terms of composition, the overall view offers a clear overview of the entire chessboard along with all the pieces, whereas the in-game perspective shows a typical opening situation of the game.
chess_block3:
Supported by the strategic use of depth of field, the complexity of the scene is reduced and the focus is directed toward the essentials. The macro shot, on the other hand, focuses on rendering the materiality and geometry.

Project at OFG
Tools: 3Ds Max & Arnold
homeoffice_intro: A home office with character: authentic materials, classic details, and a private library.
homeoffice_block1: The home office presents itself as a carefully composed workspace where aesthetic quality and functional clarity come together harmoniously. Exposed, natural brickwork creates an authentic material effect and provides a tactile contrast to the precisely crafted, dark built-in elements that define storage space and structure.
homeoffice_block2: An integrated, upholstered seating niche expands the workspace to include a place of tranquility, facilitating focused reading or informal conversations. Stucco elements accentuate the room’s proportions and lend the interior a refined, classic touch, while subtly patterned wallpaper adds depth and visual dynamism.
homeoffice_block3:
The concept is rounded out by a compact home library, which serves as both a functional addition and a design statement, lending the room an atmospheric, culturally rich identity.

Tools: 3ds Max & Arnold
dsv_intro: The new office design concept for the central service provider of the Sparkassen-Finanzgruppe – space for personal interactions and a pleasant working environment
dsv_block1:
The visualizations (lobby, cafeteria, and barista bar) offer a first glimpse into our design plans for the new headquarters of the DSV Group. The central service provider of the Sparkassen-Finanzgruppe will move into the LEED Gold-certified office complex on the W2 Campus in Stuttgart, which is currently under construction, in the course of 2026.
The design interprets the DSV Group in a modern, confident way.
dsv_block2:
New Office Design encompasses the spacious foyer, the offices, the barista bar, the cafeteria, and the landing areas such as the kitchenette and locker area on the upper floor.
Special consideration was given to the client’s desire to create more communal spaces for personal interaction. An attractive ground floor offers both employees and external visitors a more pleasant environment and places to gather.
dsv_block3:
The color-coding concept clearly distinguishes the different zones and functions through a varied color palette. Dark red tones harmonize powerfully with the DSV Group logo. Natural and durable materials underscore the timeless design and high-quality feel.

Office: Lepel & Lepel
Tools: SketchUp & Enscape
